one_line_takeaway: ARCANE shows that naively aggregating passive-beacon telemetry across campaigns via Bayesian belief updating does not beat a simple per-campaign nearest-neighbour baseline for attributing sophisticated threat actors, because such actors converge to a dense, largely indistinguishable region of a 24-dimensional behavioural fingerprint space -- and this indistinguishability is invariant to the attacker's evasion level, meaning defenders can deploy passive attribution infrastructure aggressively without fear that attacker evasion alone will neutralize it.
key_finding: Within-actor fingerprint similarity is statistically significantly higher than cross-actor similarity (mean within-actor similarity S_w=0.848 vs cross-actor S_c=0.802; separability gap delta_S=0.046; t=8.33, p=1.52e-16), confirming a theoretical basis for longitudinal attribution exists. However, ARCANE's overall attribution accuracy (30.7%) does NOT beat the per-campaign nearest-neighbour baseline (43.2%), a difference of -12.5 percentage points that is not statistically significant (p=0.087). Mean posterior confidence is also lower for ARCANE than baseline (0.137 vs 0.199, p<0.001). Per-actor accuracy peaks at 63.6% for DOUBLEPANDA and is lowest for MINTLEAF and VOIDLOTUS. Accuracy does not improve monotonically with more training campaigns (ranging 12.5%-30.7% as min_train varies from 6 down to 1). Pairwise inter-actor fingerprint similarity is uniformly high (all off-diagonal entries in [0.89, 0.99]), with same-origin actor pairs especially indistinguishable (DOUBLEPANDA/VOIDLOTUS, both CN-origin sharing Gh0st RAT and PlugX tooling, at S=0.99; LAZARUSHOUND/STORMVIPER, both KP-origin, at S=0.96). Critically, attribution accuracy is evasion-invariant: accuracy varies only between 20.6% and 25.2% across five simulated evasion levels with no statistically significant trend (F-test, p>0.05), indicating the binding constraint is feature-space separability among sophisticated actors sharing similar opsec practices, not adversarial evasion capability.
core_contribution: The paper introduces ARCANE (Attacker Re-identification via Cross-campaign Attribution Network), a probabilistic framework that aggregates passive beacon telemetry (from document/credential/source-code beacons, i.e. 'passive hack-back') across multiple campaigns and organisations to build persistent adversary fingerprints. Fingerprints are 24-dimensional vectors (infrastructure, temporal, geospatial, environment, behavioural, tool-cluster features) compared via cosine similarity, combined into a time-decayed 'Cross-Campaign Confidence' (CCC) metric, and fed into an incremental Bayesian belief-update rule (Algorithm 1) that maps CCC to actor posteriors via a linear likelihood model L(CCC)=0.50+0.45*CCC. The paper also formalizes a 'Fingerprint Separability Gap' (Definition 4) and a proposition giving the minimum separability gap needed for reliable attribution as a function of the number of training campaigns per actor. Framed explicitly as a negative/null result: the authors' own experiments show ARCANE does NOT outperform a per-campaign nearest-neighbour baseline, and they argue this null result itself is the paper's contribution, characterizing a 'separability ceiling' in behavioural fingerprint space.
threat_model: Defender-side (blue-team) attribution problem: a defender operates passive beacons (tracking payloads embedded in documents, credentials, or source code, per the authors' related 'Passive Hack-Back' framework, ref [17]) across multiple victim organisations and campaigns. When a beacon is triggered by an attacker, the defender observes telemetry (IP, TOR/VPN usage, OS, locale, timezone, tool artifacts, dwell time) and must decide which of N known threat actors (a closed set, N=8 in the experiments) is responsible, using accumulated cross-campaign evidence rather than single-incident evidence. The adversary side is modeled via an 'evasion schedule' in which each actor's evasion level (TOR usage, tool churn, locale spoofing) increases over time to simulate adaptation; the paper explicitly tests whether increasing attacker evasion degrades the defender's re-identification accuracy.
limitations: The authors state three limitations themselves: (1) Synthetic data only -- the evaluation uses a synthetic benchmark rather than real-world beacon telemetry; although actor parameters are grounded in published threat intel, real campaigns may differ distributionally, and validation on real passive hack-back deployments is left to future work. (2) Closed actor set -- the evaluation assumes a fixed, known set of N=8 actors, whereas real defenders may encounter previously unseen actors; open-set attribution is not addressed. (3) Feature dimensionality -- the 24-dimensional fingerprint is limited to what is observable from standard beacon telemetry; richer signals (HTTP header sequences, TLS fingerprints, DNS request patterns) are not incorporated and could improve separability. The paper also proposes but does not empirically validate three supplementary signal classes (victim-sector targeting, richer temporal/day-of-week clustering, infrastructure re-use graphs) that it argues could break the separability ceiling.

datasets_used: Synthetic benchmark: 8 simulated nation-state threat-actor personas (LAZARUSHOUND/KP, FROZENBEAR/RU, DOUBLEPANDA/CN, SILENTFOX/IR, MINTLEAF/KP, IRONSHARD/RU, VOIDLOTUS/CN, STORMVIPER/KP), 12 campaigns per actor over an 18-month simulated window (Jan 2024-Jun 2025), 3-8 callbacks per campaign, totaling 96 campaigns and 524 callbacks; actor parameters grounded in MITRE ATT&CK, Mandiant M-Trends 2022, and Recorded Future intelligence reports
quantitative_results: Overall attribution accuracy: ARCANE 30.7% vs baseline 43.2% (delta -12.5 percentage points, p=0.087, not significant)
Mean attribution confidence: ARCANE 0.137 vs baseline 0.199 (delta -0.062, p<0.001)
Fingerprint separability gap delta_S = 0.046 (within-actor mean similarity 0.848 vs cross-actor mean similarity 0.802), t=8.33, p=1.52e-16
Per-actor ARCANE accuracy peak: DOUBLEPANDA 63.6%; MINTLEAF and VOIDLOTUS lowest
Learning-curve accuracy range as min_train varies 6->1: 12.5% to 30.7%, non-monotonic
Pairwise inter-actor similarity: all off-diagonal entries in [0.89, 0.99]; LAZARUSHOUND/STORMVIPER S=0.96; DOUBLEPANDA/VOIDLOTUS S=0.99
Evasion robustness: accuracy 20.6%-25.2% across 5 evasion levels, F-test p>0.05 (no significant trend), n=20 trials per level
Required separability gap per Proposition 1 for N=8 actors estimated at approximately 0.08, vs the observed 0.046
baselines_compared: Per-campaign nearest-neighbour baseline: matches the current campaign's fingerprint against a static running-average profile per actor, described by the authors as representing the current state of the art in single-incident behavioural attribution
